openings.summary: Multiple continuing-contract positions for the 2026-27 school year. Requires Spanish/English bilingual proficiency and ELL/ESOL/Bilingual endorsement preferred. Salary range $83,305–$148,226.

openings.role: English Language Arts Teacher (0.6 FTE) – Long-Term Sub
openings.summary: Grades 7–8 long-term substitute from May 4–June 15, 2026. Daily pay scales: $215 (0–20 days), $230 (21–44 days), $260 (45+ days). Must hold valid WA certificate with Elementary endorsement.
General applicant pool for anticipated 2026-27 openings at middle and high-school levels. Full-time positions with district benefits; apply via Frontline.

openings.role: High School English Teacher – General Pool
openings.summary: District-wide pool for 2026-27 vacancies. Requires WA certificate with English Language Arts endorsement. Hiring occurs April–June and again in August.

openings.role: English Language Arts Teacher (1.0 FTE) – Lake Washington High School
openings.summary: Full-time position at Lake Washington High for 2026-27. Salary per teacher schedule plus LEAP days, responsibility stipends, technology stipend, and professional fund.

openings.role: High School English Teacher – Candidate Pool SY26-27
openings.summary: Multiple anticipated openings across Seattle high schools for August 1, 2026 start. Competitive salary and benefits; apply via district or third-party boards.
Full-time continuing contract for 2026-27. Application deadline June 30, 2026. Apply through BSD SchoolSpring portal.

career.notes: All districts within 30 miles of Edmonds, WA. Most postings are for the 2026-2027 school year; long-term sub roles offer immediate spring 2026 placement. Ensure WA teaching certificate and appropriate endorsements are current before applying.

family.choir: Seattle Pro Musica: auditions for 2026-27 season open in August; email auditions@seattlepromusica.org to join the mailing list. Requirements: choral experience, music-reading, foreign-language familiarity; prepare a 2-minute solo and three short choral excerpts (Bach, Brahms, Martinson) to sing a cappella in quartet. Seattle Choral Company: singers from all sections welcome year-round; informal auditions, usually vacancies every section; rehearsals Mon 7-9:30 PM, expect advanced-ensemble background. Northwest Chorale: informal sectional auditions during first weeks of Monday 7:00-9:30 PM rehearsals at Lamb of God Lutheran Church (12509 27th Ave NE); 60-member mixed choir, $75/season dues, scholarships available. Northwest Associated Arts (ChoralSounds & SilverSounds): accepting new singers [TRUNCATED]
family.toddler: Edmonds Library (Sno-Isle): Baby Storytime Tue & Thu 9 AM & 10 AM, Preschool Storytime multiple days, all free drop-in. Imagine Children’s Museum (Everett): Tue-Sun 9 AM-4 PM, children under 2 free, interactive exhibits perfect for toddlers. Woodland Park Zoo Zoomazium: indoor nature play space for 0-8 yrs, daily 9:30 AM-3 PM, puppet shows & sensory activities. Sno-Isle Libraries system also offers online storytimes and digital picture books in 70+ languages.

global_news.category: Iran War & Middle East Peace Talks
global_news.findings: US Special Envoy Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner are traveling to Pakistan for fresh direct negotiations with Iran. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi is already in Islamabad, though Iranian officials publicly deny any plan for face-to-face US talks. The discussions center on breaking the deadlock over reopening the Strait of Hormuz to commercial shipping and finding a diplomatic path to end the eight-week-old war.

The 1973 War Powers Resolution sets a hard 60-day limit on unauthorized military action, a deadline the Iran war will hit on May 1. President Trump has not sought congressional authorization and has signaled he may ignore the requirement, arguing inherent constitutional authority. Congress remains divided, with some members preparing legal challenges while others back the administration’s stance, creating a looming constitutional showdown.

Palestinians in the central Gaza Strip and the occupied West Bank are voting today in the first municipal elections in 21 years. Roughly 70,000 registered voters are choosing councils under a list system that mandates female representation. The vote proceeds despite daily Israeli attacks that have killed 12 more people in the past 24 hours and ongoing violations of the six-month ceasefire.

Weekend Edition reports that President Trump’s approval ratings have slipped as the Iran war drags into its eighth week. Congress is increasingly vocal about the lack of a clear exit strategy and the approaching War Powers deadline. Lawmakers from both parties are debating new resolutions that could either authorize continued action or force a withdrawal, reflecting growing public unease over prolonged conflict.

Created by 21-year-old Harvard dropout Avi Schiffmann, it continuously records ambient audio via an internal microphone, processes conversations in the cloud, and sends thoughtful text responses through its companion app. Battery lasts 30 hours, connects via Bluetooth, [TRUNCATED]

Core features include lesson plan generation, rubric creation, text rewriting, worksheet generators, and writing feedback tools aligned to state standards. triadic_ontology.findings:
Fresh Perspectives on the Trinity - Karl-Alber: The new "Studies on Triadic Ontology and Trinitarian Philosophy" series, edited by Markus Enders, Emmanuel Falque, John Milbank and others, explicitly bridges continental and analytic approaches to triadic thought. Volume 1, "Trinitarian Ontologies – Towards a Trinitarian Transformation of Philosophy," gathers 2021-23 conference papers that frame Trinitarian ontology as a genealogical bridge from late-antique theological transformation to 20th-century projects, while insisting that relational structures of gift, love, music and childhood fall under its remit when they exhibit triadic basic structures.

Quantum perichoresis: Quantum field theory and the Trinity - Theology and Science (2006): The paper proposes that the Trinitarian doctrine of perichoresis can be metaphorically interpreted as divine phase entanglement with the world, yielding non-local, relational holism. Christ’s kenotic self-emptying moves from the immanent perichoresis o [TRUNCATED]

ai_systems.findings:
Multi-Agent Research Systems: Google DeepMind's PaperOrchestra (April 2026) orchestrates five specialized agents—Outline, Plotting, Literature Review, Writing, and Refinement—to autonomously convert raw lab notes into submission-ready LaTeX manuscripts. Human evaluations show 50–68% improvement in literature review quality and 14–38% in overall manuscript quality. The open-source Sibyl system (March 2026) deploys 20+ agents that debate ideas, run GPU experiments, and iteratively refine papers until they meet publication standards, while also evolving its own prompts and scheduling strategies after every project.

Reasoning Models: 2026’s frontier models—GPT-5.4, Claude 4.6/4.7 Opus, Gemini 3.1 Pro—now expose explicit reasoning controls (effort level, thinking budget, parallel hypotheses). Prompt engineering has shifted from coaxing step-by-step output to configuring these built-in deliberation modes. New techniques include role+audience stacking, clarifying-questions-first, and verific [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.title: The Ferry and the Furious
newyorker.dek: A new ferry route between Edmonds and Kingston has become the unlikely flashpoint in a regional debate over transit equity, maritime labor, and the future of Puget Sound commuting.
newyorker.text:
On a recent Tuesday morning, the M/V Suquamish glided into the Edmonds ferry terminal carrying a load of commuters from Kingston, Kitsap County. The vessel—Washington State Ferries’ newest hybrid-electric ship—was making its inaugural run on a route that, until last month, didn’t exist. The Edmonds-Kingston crossing, approved by the state legislature in a late-night budget session, was pitched as a congestion-busting alternative to the clogged Highway 104 corridor. Instead, it has become a Rorschach test for the region’s anxieties about growth, labor, and who gets to move freely across Puget Sound.

At 7:12 a.m., as the Suquamish’s bow doors lowered, a small flotilla of protest kayaks bobbed in the terminal’s wake. Their occupants—members of the Puget Sound Maritime Labor Council—wielded hand-painted signs reading “Ferry Equity Now” and “Who Gets to Cross?” Their grievance: the new route bypasses existing labor contracts, using a non-union crew supplied by a private contractor, Kitsap Fast Ferry, rather than Washington State Ferries staff. “This isn’t about the boats,” said Tasha Nguyen, a deckhand on the Edmonds-Port Townsend route. “It’s about whether public transit serves the public, or just the people who can pay premium fares.”

The fare structure has indeed raised eyebrows. A one-way ticket on the Suquamish costs $8.50—nearly double the $4.50 fare on the state-run Edmonds-Port Townsend route. While Kitsap Transit subsidizes monthly passes for low-income riders, critics argue the pricing tiers create a two-tiered system. “It’s like we’re building a ferry for tech workers and retirees with boats,” said Edmonds City Councilmember Laura Johnson, who voted against the route’s approval. “Meanwhile, my constituents who clean those tech offices can’t afford to live here anymore.”

The Suquamish’s hybrid engines, touted as a green innovation, have also drawn scrutiny. The vessel’s lithium-ion battery array, manufactured by a South Korean firm, relies on cobalt sourced from the Democratic Republic of Congo—raising questions about the environmental cost of “clean” transit. “We’re just shifting emissions from Puget Sound to the Congo River basin,” said Dr. Amina Yusuf, a marine policy researcher at the University of Washington. “And the labor conditions in those mines make our ferry workers’ grievances look quaint.”

For now, the route’s future hinges on a state audit, due in June, examining whether Kitsap Fast Ferry violated public-private contracting rules. In the meantime, commuters like Michael Lang—a software engineer who moved to Edmonds last year—find themselves caught in the middle. “I just want to get to work without sitting in traffic,” Lang said, clutching a coffee from Walnut Street Coffee. “But I also don’t want to cross a picket line on water.” As the Suquamish’s horn sounded its departure, the kayakers paddled closer, their chants echoing across the glassy inlet. For a moment, the ferry’s sleek silhouette seemed less like progress and more like a question: Who is Puget Sound for?
